name: David Icke Reptilians
description: The David Icke Reptilians conspiracy theory, popularized by British author David Icke since the late 1990s, posits that a secret race of reptilian humanoids controls the world, manipulating global events and holding positions of power in governments, financial institutions, and the media. Icke claims these reptilians are descendants of an ancient alien race from the Draco constellation, who interbred with humans thousands of years ago. He identifies various prominent figures, including members of the British Royal Family, US presidents, and other influential individuals, as being reptilian in disguise or having reptilian DNA. The theory often incorporates elements of ancient astronaut theory, secret society lore, and New Age spirituality. Icke’s claims are based on his interpretation of ancient texts, personal experiences, and purported research. Mainstream science and historical scholarship overwhelmingly reject the reptilian conspiracy theory. Critics point to the lack of verifiable evidence, the reliance on anecdotal accounts and unsubstantiated claims, and the theory's potential to promote antisemitism and other forms of prejudice. The theory has been widely criticized for its lack of empirical support and its reliance on pseudoscientific reasoning. It is considered a fringe theory by the vast majority of researchers and academics. Despite the criticism, Icke's reptilian theory has gained a significant following and continues to be a prominent example of contemporary conspiracy belief.
subtitle: Secret Reptilian Rulers of the World

claims.evidence: Interpretation of ancient texts, alleged bloodline connections, claimed personal experiences.
claims.criticism: Lack of empirical evidence, reliance on anecdotal accounts, conflation of correlation with causation.

claims.claim: Prominent figures are reptilians in disguise.
claims.evidence: Physical characteristics, alleged behavior patterns, purported photographic evidence.
claims.criticism: Subjective interpretations, confirmation bias, misidentification, and unfounded speculation.

claims.claim: Reptilians manipulate global events to maintain control.
claims.evidence: Interpretation of historical events, alleged secret society involvement, purported New World Order agenda.
claims.criticism: Oversimplification of complex geopolitical dynamics, lack of verifiable evidence, promotion of distrust and paranoia.

keyFigures.name: David Icke
keyFigures.role: Main proponent and popularizer of the theory

keyFigures.name: Richard Dawkins
keyFigures.role: Prominent critic, highlights lack of scientific basis

timeline.event: David Icke begins promoting the Reptilian conspiracy theory.
The theory gains popularity online and through Icke's books and lectures.

sources.author: Joseph P. Laycock
sources.work: Dangerous Ideas: A Brief History of Conspiracy Theories in the United States
sources.title: Joseph P. Laycock
